title: Bulgaria în zona euro: cum se raportează românii și cum putem reseta relația dintre noi
summary: The article examines Bulgaria's successful adoption of the euro on January 1, 2026, and how this milestone has affected Romanian-Bulgarian relations. It explores the mix of jealousy, schadenfreude, and admiration among Romanian elites, while arguing that both countries would benefit from deeper cooperation rather than blind competition. The author calls for resetting stereotypes rooted in Cold War-era perceptions and building a genuine win-win economic relationship.
talking_points: Romania's elites and business interests pragmatically avoid eurozone accession to escape stricter fiscal controls, not because Romania is incapable of joining
Bulgaria's conservative fiscal policy and currency board regime enabled its euro adoption, while Romania's consumption-driven economy and high budget deficit delay its path by at least 5-7 years
Romanian media sought negative narratives about Bulgaria's euro transition, reflecting a broader schadenfreude attitude between the two nations
Both countries stand to gain from mutual economic success — a win-win dynamic where Bulgaria benefits from wealthier Romanian consumers and Romania benefits from Bulgaria's positive euro experience
Deep-seated Romanian stereotypes portray Bulgarians as untrustworthy Russian agents, a Cold War-era prejudice that hinders meaningful bilateral cooperation
author: Vladimir Mitev
